questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a history of inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction three days ago develops acute dyspnoea and hypotension. On examination, he has cool peripheries, raised jugular venous pressure, and a loud pansystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla. A new thrill is palpable. Chest radiograph shows pulmonary oedema. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute mitral regurgitation due to papillary muscle rupture
Acute aortic regurgitation due to aortic dissection
questions.explanation: The loud pansystolic murmur at apex with thrill, in the context of recent inferior MI, is classic for acute MR from papillary muscle rupture. Ventricular septal rupture typically has a harsh holosystolic murmur at left lower sternal border, not isolated apical. Acute aortic reg has a diastolic murmur.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with palpitations and occasional dyspnoea on exertion. She is otherwise well. On auscultation, there is a mid-systolic click followed by a late systolic murmur at the apex. Echocardiogram shows prolapse of the mitral valve leaflets with mild mitral regurgitation. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The mid-systolic click and late systolic murmur are classic for MVP. HCM has a systolic murmur that increases with Valsalva; aortic stenosis is a mid-systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur; ASD has a fixed split S2 and pulmonary flow murmur; TR has a pansystolic murmur at left sternal border.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with ischaemic cardiomyopathy and a left ventricular ejection fraction of 30% presents with worsening shortness of breath and peripheral oedema. On examination, he has a soft, blowing pansystolic murmur at the apex that does not radiate to the axilla. A third heart sound is present. He has bibasal crackles. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Dilated cardiomyopathy without significant valvular disease
questions.explanation: In a patient with dilated LV and HFrEF, a soft apical pansystolic murmur without radiation suggests functional MR due to annular dilatation. Primary MR typically has a louder murmur radiating to axilla. Dilated cardiomyopathy without MR would not have a murmur. Constrictive pericarditis has Kussmaul's sign and a pericardial knock.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with known myxomatous mitral valve disease presents with acute onset of severe breathlessness and orthopnoea over the past hour. She is in extremis, with blood pressure 85/50 mmHg, heart rate 120 bpm, and oxygen saturations 88% on high-flow oxygen. She has a loud pansystolic murmur at the apex with a palpable thrill. Chest radiograph shows severe pulmonary oedema. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute aortic regurgitation due to endocarditis
Acute mitral regurgitation due to chordal rupture
Acute decompensated heart failure with preserved ejection fraction
questions.explanation: Acute severe breathlessness and shock in a patient with known myxomatous disease and a new loud apical murmur with thrill points to chordal rupture causing flail leaflet and acute MR. Infective endocarditis typically has fever and may present more subacutely. Pneumonia would have focal signs and fever. HFpEF would not have such a loud murmur and thrill.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with a three-week history of hoarseness. He has no cough, dysphagia, or weight loss. He has a history of chronic severe mitral regurgitation due to mitral valve prolapse, but has been relatively asymptomatic. On examination, he has a holosystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla, and the apical impulse is displaced to the left. Indirect laryngoscopy shows left vocal cord palsy. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Aortic aneurysm causing recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy
questions.explanation: In patients with chronic severe MR, the enlarged left atrium can compress the left recurrent laryngeal nerve against the aortic arch or pulmonary ligament, causing left vocal cord palsy (Ortner's syndrome). This is an atypical presentation. The other options would require additional signs or risk factors; the known history of severe MR makes this the most likely.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with gradually worsening dyspnoea on exertion over 6 months. She has no chest pain or palpitations. On examination, the apex beat is displaced laterally, S1 is soft, and there is a pan-systolic murmur heard loudest at the apex radiating to the axilla. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: TTE with Doppler is the first-line investigation for confirming mitral regurgitation (MR) and assessing its severity. ECG and CXR may show supportive features but are not diagnostic. TOE is reserved for when TTE is inadequate or for detailed anatomy in planning surgery, not for initial diagnosis. Cardiac MRI is an alternative but not first-line.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known moderate mitral regurgitation (MR) attends for routine follow-up. He is asymptomatic. Which investigation is most appropriate to quantify the severity of his MR?
options: Transthoracic echocardiography with colour flow Doppler
questions.explanation: TTE with colour flow Doppler and quantitative parameters (vena contracta width, PISA radius, regurgitant volume/fraction) is the standard method for grading MR severity. TOE is more invasive and reserved for specific indications. Cardiac MRI is accurate but not routinely used first-line. Exercise echo assesses functional capacity but not directly severity. CT coronary angiography is for coronary assessment, not MR quantification.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man presents with acute inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction. He becomes hypotensive 24 hours later, with raised jugular venous pressure and a new pan-systolic murmur heard both at the apex and left lower sternal edge. Which investigation should be performed immediately to confirm the cause?
questions.explanation: In acute MR due to papillary muscle rupture, bedside TTE is the first-line test to quickly confirm the diagnosis and assess severity. It can also help differentiate from ventricular septal rupture. TOE is reserved if TTE is nondiagnostic. ECG and CXR are not diagnostic. Left ventriculography is invasive and not immediate.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with fever, weight loss, and a new murmur of mitral regurgitation. Blood cultures are positive for Streptococcus viridans. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of infective endocarditis and assess the valve?
questions.explanation: TOE is superior to TTE for detecting vegetations, especially on the mitral valve, and is the investigation of choice when endocarditis is suspected with a positive blood culture. TTE may be negative in up to 30% of cases. Cardiac MRI and PET-CT are not first-line for vegetations. CT chest is not helpful.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with severe COPD and known chronic mitral regurgitation complains of worsening dyspnoea. A transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) provides suboptimal images due to poor acoustic windows. Which investigation is most appropriate next to assess his MR severity?
questions.explanation: When TTE is inadequate in a patient with suspected severe MR, TOE is the next best option for detailed assessment of valve morphology and severity. Contrast-enhanced TTE may improve endocardial definition but does not fully overcome poor windows for Doppler. Cardiac MRI is an alternative if TOE is contraindicated, but TOE is more readily available and provides real-time assessment. Exercise echo and CT angiography are not appropriate for MR severity quantification in this setting.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known severe mitral regurgitation due to posterior leaflet prolapse presents with worsening dyspnoea (NYHA III) despite diuretics. His LVEF is 60% and he has no significant comorbidities. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
questions.explanation: In degenerative mitral regurgitation, mitral valve repair is preferred over replacement when anatomically feasible, as it preserves left ventricular function and has lower operative mortality. MitraClip is reserved for patients at high surgical risk; this patient is a good surgical candidate.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with acute pulmonary oedema 3 days after an inferior myocardial infarction. Echocardiography reveals severe mitral regurgitation due to a flail posterior leaflet from papillary muscle rupture. He is in cardiogenic shock. Which is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
questions.explanation: Acute severe mitral regurgitation from papillary muscle rupture requires urgent surgical intervention (repair or replacement). Intra-aortic balloon pump is a temporary bridge, not definitive.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with asymptomatic severe mitral regurgitation (degenerative) is found to have an LV ejection fraction of 50% on routine echocardiography. She is in sinus rhythm. What is the most appropriate management?
questions.explanation: According to NICE guidelines, surgery is indicated in asymptomatic severe MR when LVEF is <60% or when left ventricular end-systolic diameter >45 mm. Repair is preferred.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with chronic severe mitral regurgitation presents with acute dyspnoea and hypoxia. Chest X-ray shows pulmonary oedema. Blood pressure is 100/60 mmHg. Which of the following treatments is contraindicated in this acute setting?
questions.explanation: Beta-blockers can worsen acute decompensated heart failure by reducing contractility and increasing regurgitant volume. Afterload reduction (GTN) and diuresis are preferred; IABP and surgery may be considered.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old pregnant woman (28 weeks gestation) with known moderate mitral regurgitation presents with worsening dyspnoea and peripheral oedema. She is in sinus rhythm. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, afterload reduction with hydralazine and diuresis with furosemide are safe and effective for symptomatic MR. ACE inhibitors are teratogenic; surgery is reserved for refractory cases.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known mild mitral regurgitation presents to ED with acute onset dyspnoea and orthopnoea. On exam, BP 100/60, HR 110, respiratory rate 28, O2 sat 88% on 15L O2. Crackles up to mid-zones. Loud pansystolic murmur at apex. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
questions.explanation: In acute severe MR with pulmonary oedema, immediate diuresis with IV furosemide is the priority to relieve respiratory distress. Urgent echo is needed but after initial stabilisation.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman is found to have severe mitral regurgitation on echocardiogram performed for an unrelated murmur. She is asymptomatic, with normal exercise tolerance. ECG shows sinus rhythm. TTE: severe MR with LVEF 65%, LVESD 35mm. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer for mitral valve repair surgery
questions.explanation: In asymptomatic severe primary MR with preserved LV function (LVEF >60%, LVESD <40mm), NICE recommends annual surveillance echocardiography. ACEi or beta-blockers are not indicated without hypertension or LV dysfunction.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known mitral valve prolapse and mild regurgitation presents with a 5-day history of fever, chills, and new holosystolic murmur. Temp 38.5°C, BP 110/70. No signs of heart failure. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Start intravenous antibiotics (e.g., vancomycin and gentamicin)
Obtain two sets of blood cultures
questions.explanation: Suspected infective endocarditis mandates blood cultures (minimum 2 sets) before antibiotics to maximise diagnostic yield. Antibiotics should be started only after cultures; echo is essential but secondary.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman at 30 weeks gestation with known moderate mitral regurgitation presents to the maternity assessment unit with worsening breathlessness over the last week. She has no chest pain. O2 sat 96% on air, BP 110/70, HR 90. Lungs clear. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: New breathlessness in pregnancy with known MR requires urgent echocardiography to reassess severity and rule out alternate causes. Diuretics may be needed if pulmonary congestion, but imaging guides management first.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known chronic severe mitral regurgitation presents to the ED with palpitations and lightheadedness for 2 hours. ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 145 bpm. BP 105/70, HR irregularly irregular, no signs of heart failure. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
questions.explanation: Acute AF with rapid ventricular rate in a haemodynamically stable patient requires immediate rate control. IV beta-blockers (e.g., bisoprolol) are first-line. Anticoagulation comes after rate control and risk assessment.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with hypertension presents with sudden-onset severe dyspnoea and orthopnoea. He has a pansystolic murmur at the apex. Which clinical sign would raise suspicion for acute severe mitral regurgitation?
questions.explanation: In acute severe MR, the left atrium is non-compliant, producing a prominent S3 gallop due to rapid early left ventricular filling. A displaced apex beat suggests chronic volume overload, and wide splitting of S2 is not characteristic.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with asymptomatic severe mitral regurgitation from myxomatous disease has an echocardiogram showing LVEF 62% and LVESD 38 mm. Which finding would be a red flag for early left ventricular systolic dysfunction?
questions.explanation: In chronic severe MR, asymptomatic LV systolic dysfunction is defined by LVEF <60%. This threshold prompts surgical referral to prevent irreversible LV damage. LVESD >40 mm is also concerning, but LVEF decline is the established trigger (option A is explicitly below 60%).

questions.stem: A 45-year-old intravenous drug user presents with fever and a new murmur of mitral regurgitation. Transoesophageal echocardiography shows a mobile vegetation on the anterior mitral leaflet. Which feature most increases the risk of systemic embolisation?
questions.explanation: Vegetation size >10 mm on the mitral valve is associated with a higher risk of embolic events, particularly if the vegetation is mobile. The size threshold is the most predictive factor among the options.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old pregnant woman at 32 weeks gestation with known mitral valve prolapse and mild MR develops sudden dyspnoea and orthopnoea. There is a new pansystolic murmur. Which finding confirms that she has decompensated?
questions.explanation: In the setting of acute severe MR, pulmonary oedema on chest X-ray is definitive evidence of left atrial pressure elevation and acute decompensation, requiring immediate intervention. While S3 gallop and elevated JVP may be present, pulmonary oedema is the most objective emergency.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with an acute inferior STEMI develops sudden breathlessness and hypotension on day 2. A loud pansystolic murmur is heard. Which echocardiographic finding would confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In the context of acute MI, a new murmur and haemodynamic compromise strongly suggest papillary muscle rupture. A flail leaflet on echo is pathognomonic for chordal rupture, distinguishing it from other causes of acute MR.
